outline.heading: Availability and requirements
outline.preview: # Availability and requirements AMMOS is written in C and python and runs on Linux platforms. It is also operational on MacOSX system. Detailed installation instructions are provided in the software package. The source code of AMMOS is freely available under the terms and conditions of the GNU Public License from http://www.vls3d.com/.

reading_plan.reading_goal: Reconstruct the research evolution trajectory of AMMOS: identify the problem (limitations of virtual screening), constraints (speed, accuracy, open-source), hypotheses (MM minimization improves docking results), design choices (AMMP force fields, minimization protocols, rescoring), computational experiments (small molecule minimization, protein-ligand complex refinement, enrichment studies), observations (improved enrichment, better pose prediction), failures/partial results (some force fields underperform, flexible receptor cases not always beneficial), revisions (protocol adjustments), validations (comparison with other force fields, enrichment metrics), and limitations (force field accuracy, computational cost).

must_find: Problem statement: limitations of current virtual screening tools (e.g., lack of structural optimization, scoring inaccuracies).
Hypotheses: MM minimization of ligands and complexes improves binding pose prediction and enrichment.
Design choices: selection of AMMP force fields (sp4, sp5), minimization algorithms (conjugate gradient, etc.), and rescoring function.
Experimental setup: compound collections, protein targets (ER, NA), docking protocol (DOCK6), enrichment metrics (EF, AUC).
Observations: improved enrichment with AMMOS minimization and rescoring, especially for hydrophobic pockets; better pose prediction for some cases.
Failures/partial results: sp5 force field underperforms; flexible receptor minimization (Case 1,2) not always beneficial; some actives still misranked.
Revisions: protocol adjustments (e.g., using only heavy atoms for RMSD, limiting iterations).
Validations: comparison with Tff and MMFF94s force fields; enrichment studies with decoy sets.
Limitations: force field accuracy, computational cost, need for further tuning, limited to two protein targets.

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Virtual screening tools lack structural optimization of compounds and receptor flexibility, leading to inaccurate binding pose prediction and scoring. AMMOS addresses this by providing automatic MM minimization of compound libraries and pre-docked protein-ligand complexes to improve enrichment and pose accuracy.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: AMMOS is an open-source tool for refining 3D structures of small molecules and energy minimizing protein-ligand complexes using AMMP. It improves enrichment in virtual screening, finding 40-60% of actives in top 3-5% of database after post-docking minimization.
process_signals: Problem: structural optimization, receptor flexibility, accurate prediction of interactions not easily addressed by available programs.
Hypothesis: post-docking optimization improves efficiency of SBVLS.
Result: AMMOS improved enrichment after pre-docking stage.
metrics: Enrichment factor: % actives in top % of database

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: SBVLS faces challenges: receptor flexibility, accurate binding energy prediction, ranking compounds, and library quality. Post-docking optimization via MM can improve pose and scoring. AMMOS uses AMMP for fast, automatic minimization of large compound sets.
process_signals: Problem: receptor flexibility, scoring inaccuracies, library quality.
Prior work: post-docking optimization improves enrichment.
Design choice: AMMP chosen for speed, multipole method, robust optimizers, and open-source license.
failures_or_limits: Full conformational sampling not feasible for large number of complexes.
MM cannot explore large conformational changes.

section_gists.heading: AMMP Molecular Mechanics
section_gists.gist: AMMP force fields sp4 and sp5 are based on UFF and AMBER charges. sp5 is an improved version with better electrostatics. Minimization methods tested: steepest descent, conjugate gradient, BFGS, simplex, genetic algorithm.
process_signals: Design choice: sp4 and sp5 force fields.
Method selection: tested multiple minimizers to find most efficient.
methods: Minimization methods: steepest descent, conjugate gradient, BFGS, simplex, GA

section_gists.heading: Energy minimization of small organic molecules using AMMOS
section_gists.gist: Tested sp4 and sp5 on four molecules. Conjugate gradient and GA gave lowest energies; conjugate gradient chosen for speed. sp4 performed slightly better than sp5. Protocol: 2x500 iterations, convergence 0.02 kcal/mol/Å.
process_signals: Hypothesis: sp5 should be better, but sp4 gave slightly lower energies.
Decision: conjugate gradient with sp4 selected for automatic procedure.
Protocol development: 2x500 iterations as compromise between optimization and speed.
failures_or_limits: sp5 underperformed sp4 for some molecules.
Steepest descent and simplex gave higher energies.

section_gists.heading: Docking and scoring protocol
section_gists.gist: Multi-step docking with DOCK6: rigid body docking with MS-DOCK, then flexible docking with anchor-first algorithm. Used contact scoring then DOCK energy score. Saved up to 10 poses per ligand for AMMOS minimization.
process_signals: Experimental setup: two-stage VLS to reduce number of complexes for minimization.
Parameter choices: bump overlap 0.75 for NA, 0.50 for ER based on previous observations.
methods: Contact scoring and DOCK energy score

section_gists.heading: Algorithm of AMMOS
section_gists.gist: AMMOS automates minimization of pre-docked complexes with five flexibility cases (fully flexible to rigid receptor). Uses PREAMMP and AMMP autolink. Minimization: 2x500 iterations conjugate gradient. Rescoring by AMMP interaction energy.
process_signals: Design choice: five flexibility cases to balance accuracy and speed.
Default protocol: 2x500 iterations conjugate gradient.

section_gists.heading: Implementation of AMMOS
section_gists.gist: Implemented in C and Python, uses AMMP and PREAMMP. Handles large ligand sets via parallel processing. sp5 force field created from literature parameters. Execution times: 0.3s per small molecule, 24s-5min per complex depending on flexibility.
process_signals: Technical constraint: long execution times for full flexibility; parallel mode implemented.
sp5 not natively in AMMP; created from reference parameters.
failures_or_limits: Computational cost high for full flexibility (case 1: 5 min per complex).

section_gists.heading: Energy minimization of small molecules with AMMOS
section_gists.gist: Conjugate gradient with sp4 chosen as default. 2x500 iterations sufficient. Nonbon potential selected for speed. sp4 gave slightly lower energies than sp5 for some molecules.
process_signals: Observation: conjugate gradient and GA best, but GA slower.
Decision: sp4 over sp5 due to better energy reduction.
Protocol: 2x500 iterations, nonbon potential.
failures_or_limits: sp5 did not outperform sp4 as expected.
Steepest descent failed to resolve clashes.

section_gists.heading: Energy minimization of small molecules with other force fields
section_gists.gist: AMMOS (sp4) compared to MMFF94s and Tff. Similar energy reduction and RMSD from initial structures. RMSD from X-ray structures comparable across force fields, validating AMMOS.
process_signals: Validation: AMMOS results similar to established force fields.
Observation: RMSD from X-ray ~1-2 Å, acceptable for docking.
methods: Comparison with MMFF94s and Tff force fields
metrics: RMSD from initial and X-ray structures

section_gists.heading: Enrichment studies using AMMOS
section_gists.gist: AMMOS minimization improved enrichment for ER (cases 3,4 better than 5) and NA (case 5 slightly better). Pose RMSD <1.5 Å for most inhibitors. Full flexibility (case 1,2) tested only on actives due to cost.
process_signals: Observation: partial flexibility (cases 3,4) best for ER; rigid receptor (case 5) best for NA.
Failure: full flexibility not always beneficial; NA prefers rigid receptor.
Limitation: case 1,2 too expensive for full library.
methods: Enrichment studies with AMMOS minimization cases 1-5
metrics: Enrichment: % actives in top % of database
failures_or_limits: Case 1,2 not tested on full library due to computational cost.
NA enrichment only modest; rigid receptor slightly better.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: AMMOS addresses need for open-source post-processing tool in SBVLS. It refines compound structures, optimizes binding poses, and rescores. Useful in hierarchical VLS protocols.
process_signals: Motivation: open-source tool for MM optimization in screening.
Application: pre-docking minimization and post-docking refinement.
failures_or_limits: Force field accuracy may affect results.

section_gists.heading: Structural refinement of the compound collections with AMMOS
section_gists.gist: Pre-docking minimization may improve docking accuracy. Ligands bind in low-energy states, so minimizing strain energy is beneficial. AMMOS can automatically minimize large compound collections.
process_signals: Hypothesis: minimizing compounds before docking improves results.
Preliminary evidence: docking ER inhibitors minimized with AMMOS improved accuracy.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Post-docking MM minimization improves enrichment and pose prediction in SBVLS.
Pre-docking minimization of compound libraries enhances docking accuracy.
Partial receptor flexibility during minimization is sufficient and more efficient than full flexibility.
AMMP sp4 force field with conjugate gradient provides a good balance of speed and accuracy for large-scale screening.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why sp5 underperformed sp4 despite being an improved version.
Whether pre-docking minimization consistently improves enrichment across diverse targets.
Optimal balance between receptor flexibility and computational cost.
Generalizability to other protein targets and docking programs.

events.summary: Virtual screening programs do not easily address structural optimization of compounds, receptor flexibility, and accurate prediction of protein-ligand interactions.
Post-docking optimization and structural refinement of compounds can improve the efficiency of structure-based virtual screening.
AMMOS was developed to automatically refine 3D structures of small molecules and energy minimize protein-ligand complexes using AMMP.
AMMOS was evaluated by comparing minimized small molecule structures with those from Tripos and MMFF94s force fields.
AMMOS was used for full flexible minimization of protein-ligand complexes from a multi-step virtual screening, and enrichment was assessed.
AMMOS improved enrichment, finding 40-60% of actives in the top 3-5% of the database after pre-docking stage.
Full conformational sampling is not feasible for a large number of protein-ligand complexes due to exponential rise in conformations.
AMMOS uses AMMP for fast energy minimization due to its fast multipole method and robust optimizers.

events.summary: Conjugate gradient succeeded to overcome clashes where steepest descent failed.

evidence_bank.quote: available computer programs do not easily address problems such as: structural optimization of compounds in a screening library, receptor flexibility/induced-fit, and accurate prediction of protein-ligand interactions

It has been shown that structural optimization of chemical compounds and that post-docking optimization in multi-step structure-based virtual screening approaches help to further improve the overall efficiency of the methods.

we developed the program AMMOS for refining both, the 3D structures of the small molecules present in chemical libraries and the predicted receptor-ligand complexes through allowing partial to full atom flexibility through molecular mechanics optimization.
The performance of our package was evaluated by comparing the structures of small chemical entities minimized by AMMOS with those minimized with the Tripos and MMFF94s force fields.

AMMOS was used for full flexible minimization of protein-ligands complexes obtained from a mutli-step virtual screening. Enrichment studies of the selected pre-docked complexes containing 60% of the initially added inhibitors were carried out with or without final AMMOS minimization on two protein targets having different binding pocket properties.
AMMOS was able to improve the enrichment after the pre-docking stage with 40 to 60% of the initially added active compounds found in the top 3% to 5% of the entire compound collection.
the full sampling of all possible conformations is not feasible for a large number of protein-ligand complexes
AMMOS proposes relatively fast energy minimization by making use of the full-featured molecular mechanics program AMMP

AMMP has several advantages that make it relevant for the present applications such as: a fast multipole method for including all atoms in the calculation of long-range potentials and robust structural optimizers.
Table 1 and Additional file 1 present the results for the four molecules and the five different applied optimization methods.
conjugate gradient and GA produced lowest energies, however GA was much slower due to its stochastic nature.
For all tested molecules, steepest descent and simplex search led to minimized structures with higher energies.

Although sp5 has been reported to be an improved version of sp4 [39], in our work sp4 minimized slightly better than sp5 in terms of energy differences between the initial and minimized structure, in particular for thymidine and raloxifene.
Following the above observations, we chose conjugate gradient and sp4 as the minimization protocol for subsequent assessment of AMMOS.
We also minimized structures containing important internal clashes in order to assess the robustness of the approach.
execution times when a large number of ligands should be minimized with AMMOS can be very long.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Virtual screening tools lack structural optimization of compounds and receptor flexibility, leading to inaccurate binding pose prediction and scoring.

canonical_events.summary: Virtual screening programs do not easily address structural optimization of compounds, receptor flexibility, and accurate prediction of protein-ligand interactions.
Post-docking optimization and structural refinement of compounds can improve the efficiency of structure-based virtual screening.
AMMOS was developed to automatically refine 3D structures of small molecules and energy minimize protein-ligand complexes using AMMP.
Tested AMMOS minimization on four small molecules using sp4 and sp5 force fields with five optimization methods.
Conjugate gradient and GA produced lowest energies, but GA was slower; steepest descent and simplex gave higher energies.
sp4 minimized slightly better than sp5 in terms of energy differences, contrary to expectation that sp5 is improved.
Chose conjugate gradient with sp4 as the default minimization protocol for AMMOS, with 2×500 iterations and nonbon potential.
AMMOS results are close to those obtained by MMFF94s and Tff force fields, confirming ability to remove ligand energy strains.
AMMOS minimization applied to a chemical library of 37970 molecules; 76% had ΔE below 50 kcal/mol.
AMMOS was used for full flexible minimization of protein-ligand complexes from a multi-step virtual screening, and enrichment was assessed.
AMMOS minimization improved enrichment for ER with partial flexibility (cases 3,4) and for NA with rigid receptor (case 5).
After AMMOS rescoring, 40% of NA inhibitors and 60% of ER actives were found in top 5% of compound libraries.
Post-docking MM minimization can worsen enrichment for some proteins, e.g., NA and thymidine kinase, possibly due to large pockets with small ligands.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Virtual screening programs lack structural optimization and receptor flexibility, leading to inaccurate predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether post-docking optimization can improve enrichment in structure-based virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Post-docking optimization and structural refinement of compounds can improve the efficiency of structure-based virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an automated tool for post-docking minimization of protein-ligand complexes.

evidence.quote_or_span: available computer programs do not easily address problems such as: structural optimization of compounds in a screening library, receptor flexibility/induced-fit, and accurate prediction of protein-ligand interactions

evidence.quote_or_span: we developed the program AMMOS for refining both, the 3D structures of the small molecules present in chemical libraries and the predicted receptor-ligand complexes through allowing partial to full atom flexibility through molecular mechanics optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: we developed the program AMMOS for refining both, the 3D structures of the small molecules present in chemical libraries and the predicted receptor-ligand complexes through allowing partial to full atom flexibility through molecular mechanics optimization.
AMMOS proposes relatively fast energy minimization by making use of the full-featured molecular mechanics program AMMP

AMMP has several advantages that make it relevant for the present applications such as: a fast multipole method for including all atoms in the calculation of long-range potentials and robust structural optimizers.

success_verification.final_conclusion: AMMOS improves enrichment in multi-step SBVLS, retrieving 40-60% of actives in top 3-5%, but may worsen enrichment for targets with large pockets and small ligands; solvation effects not considered.
limitations: Post-docking minimization can worsen enrichment for some proteins (e.g., NA, thymidine kinase) due to large pockets with small ligands.
AMMOS does not consider solvation effects; future release planned.
